answer:
In the ghazals comprising *Diwan-E-Ghalib* and *Diwan-E-Zauq*, the **maqta** functions as the structural closing couplet (*sher*) of the poem.[^1] It conventionally concludes the ghazal and typically incorporates the poet's *takhallus* (pen name)—*Ghalib* or *Asad* for Mirza Ghalib, and *Zauq* for Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq—serving as a signature that personalizes the finale, often with self-referential irony, lament, or summation.[^2] This unit provides closure after the radif-repeating body, distinguishing it from earlier *matla*-opening or intervening couplets.

### Examples from the Corpus

**Ghalib's *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, Ghazal 149:**  
The maqta explicitly names "Ghalib" while depicting poetic restraint amid emotional overflow:  
> I had to stop Ghalib last night, or you would have seen it all;  
> in his flood of tears, the sky was just a handful of the squall.[^3]

**Ghalib's *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, Ghazal 88:**  
One maqta-like instance invokes "Ghalib" amid themes of artistic thirst (not the absolute final couplet, but a signed pivot):  
> O Ghalib, themes of thirst could not be penned with art,  
> Though to the endless river of my open heart,  
> A limiting and lonely shore was tied.[^4]

**Zauq's *Diwan-E-Zauq*, Ghazal 5:**  
The terminal maqta signs with "Zauq," capturing surrender to the beloved's poise:  
> Ah Zauq, I fell, and clutched my heart, its every door ajar,  
> when she just stood, with such a grace, and on her hip she laid a hand.[^5]

**Zauq's *Diwan-E-Zauq*, Ghazal 16:**  
The maqta closes by contrasting lovelessness with "Zauq"'s inner fire:  
> O Zauq, a heart that has no fire of love  
> Is worse than stone, which has a fiery core, at least.[^6]

**Ghalib's *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, Ghazal 4:**  
"Asad" (Ghalib's alternate takhallus) appears in a near-final couplet evoking fiery passion:  
> Asad, consumed by waiting's restless, burning might,  
> A moth before the flame of sacred light was.[^7]

The English translations do not define *maqta* explicitly (transliteration variants retain the Urdu term incidentally, e.g., "Maqta_silsila-e_shauq" in Ghazal 195, but without gloss).[^8] These examples illustrate its role empirically from the verse structure.

[^1]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 4, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/04-aaina-dekh-apna-sa-munh-le-ke-reh-gaye/translation/). Cf. *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 5, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/05-ae-zauq-waqt-naale-ke-rakh-le-jigar-pe-haath/translation/).
[^2]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 149, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/149-naala-e-dil-mein-shab-andaaz-e-asar-naayaab-tha/translation/#p-f26b94).
[^3]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 149, ¶ [p-f26b94](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/149-naala-e-dil-mein-shab-andaaz-e-asar-naayaab-tha/translation/#p-f26b94).
[^4]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 88, ¶ [p-422ffb](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/88-jab-ba-taqreeb-e-safar-yaar-ne-mehmil-baandha/translation/#p-422ffb).
[^5]: *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 5, ¶ [p-938be9](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/05-ae-zauq-waqt-naale-ke-rakh-le-jigar-pe-haath/translation/#p-938be9).
[^6]: *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 16, ¶ [p-df8cda](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/16-ek-sadma-dard-e-dil-se-meri-jaan-par-toh-hai/translation/#p-df8cda).
[^7]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 4, ¶ [p-d266ce](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/04-aaina-dekh-apna-sa-munh-le-ke-reh-gaye/translation/#p-d266ce).
[^8]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 195, [transliteration](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/195-waan-pahunch-kar-jo-ghash-aata-paye-ham-hai-hum-ko/transliteration/).
